nativeRange.habitat: Surge zone of clear seaward reef flats; benthopelagic, typically at depths of roughly 0–10 m where it schools over reef platforms exposed to wave action and strong currents

decorPreferences: large amounts of live rock for grazing

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer on filamentous and calcareous algae in the wild. In aquaria, dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based prepared foods are generally advised at least three times per week to support immunity and reduce aggression. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis, brine shrimp, and other meaty foods alongside tankmates, but algae should remain the dietary staple. Adequate algae availability is commonly reported to reduce aggression and lower the risk of Head and Lateral Line Erosion.

avoidWith: similarly shaped or similarly coloured fish in undersized systems

summary: An Indo-Pacific surgeonfish from shallow reef surge zones, distinguished by a grey body dotted with white spots, two silver bars on the forebody, and yellow tail and pelvic fins. Generally advised for intermediate keepers with large, well-flowed systems; have been known to be aggressive toward other tangs and similarly shaped fish.
careNotes:
The Mustard Guttatus Tang (Acanthurus guttatus), also widely known as the Whitespotted Surgeonfish, is a mid-sized Indo-Pacific acanthurid that inhabits the surge zones of clear, exposed seaward reef flats. In the wild it forms schools over shallow reef platforms (typically 0–10 m), grazing on filamentous and calcareous algae. Adults are generally reported at around 22–30 cm in captivity, with FishBase listing a wild maximum of 26 cm TL and LiveAquaria citing up to 12 inches.

A system of at least roughly 700 L (around 180+ US gallons) with a long footprint and abundant open swimming space is generally advised; retail sources span 180–250 US gal as starting points, with experienced keepers commonly recommending substantially larger systems for adults. Strong flow and high oxygenation help replicate the surge-zone habitat. Stable reef conditions (around 24–27°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12) are commonly suggested.

Diet should be predominantly algae-based. Dried marine seaweed (nori) on a clip, spirulina-rich prepared foods, and grazable algae on live rock are generally considered the staple; meaty foods such as mysis can be offered as supplements. Like other tangs, this species has been reported as susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion when water quality slips or the diet is protein-heavy, and quarantine is generally advised due to a documented susceptibility to ich and other skin parasites.

The species is moderately aggressive: it has been reported as peaceful toward most non-tang reef fish but aggressive toward conspecifics and other surgeonfishes. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per system and to introduce it last after smaller and more timid tankmates have established. Like all acanthurids, it has a sharp caudal-peduncle spine ('scalpel') used in defence — FishBase classifies caudal spines in this genus as venomous, and handlers are advised to use caution during transfers.

Source cross-check notes: Adult size — FishBase 26 cm TL; LiveAquaria 12 in (≈30 cm); Quality Marine ≈10 in (≈25 cm); range averaged to 22–30 cm. Temperature — LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C) vs FishBase 25–29.3°C; averaged to 24–27°C. Tank minimum — Quality Marine 180 US gal (≈680 L), LiveAquaria 250 US gal (≈946 L), with experienced keepers cited as using 400+ US gal; biased toward the larger side per the active-swimmer / surge-zone context and rounded to 700 L (nearest allowed nice-value at or above the source average).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of the Mustard Guttatus Tang is not documented in available aquarium-hobby literature. In the wild the species forms schools in surge-zone reef habitat and is presumed to spawn pelagically like other Acanthurus species, but reproduction in home aquaria is considered effectively unattainable given the tank volumes and pelagic larval rearing required.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for the Mustard Guttatus Tang. Provided adult size (12 in), minimum tank size (250 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.021–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (Moderate), temperament (semi-aggressive toward other tangs and surgeonfish, peaceful with others), reef compatibility (with caution), herbivorous diet, and the recommendation for live rock and marine seaweed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (26 cm TL max), native range (Indo-Pacific from East African oceanic islands to Hawaiian, Marquesan and Tuamoto islands; north to Ryukyu, south to New Caledonia and Rapa), depth (0–10 m), temperature (25–29.3°C, mean 28.2°C), surge-zone reef-flat habitat, herbivorous diet on filamentous and calcareous algae, schooling behaviour, and the genus' sharp forward-pointing caudal spine (classified as venomous in Acanthurus).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (nearly 10 in), minimum tank size (180 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH), moderate care level, semi-aggressive temperament toward similarly shaped tangs, reef-compatible status with caveat about picking at sessile inverts when underfed, and varied diet leaning algae-based.

additional.name: Reef Builders
additional.notes: Hobbyist species spotlight confirming surge-zone habitat, schooling behaviour over shallow reef platforms, and the distinctive white-spot / silver-bar / yellow-tail coloration of Acanthurus guttatus.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records are documented in available aquarium-hobby literature. Wild reproduction is presumed pelagic, similar to other Acanthurus species. Home-aquarium reproduction is considered effectively unattainable.
